title: Spaghetti al Pomodoro

subs.name: rice vermicelli or thin wheat noodles
subs.notes: boil for 3–4 minutes less; sauce quantity stays the same

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes, roughly chopped
visual_checks: or one 400 g can of whole peeled tomatoes
ingredients.notes: or one 400 g can of whole peeled tomatoes

subs.name: 400 g canned crushed tomatoes
subs.notes: use San Marzano style if available; adjust salt accordingly

subs.name: any neutral cooking oil
subs.notes: olive oil adds authentic flavour but refined sunflower oil works

subs.name: fresh coriander leaves or dried oregano
subs.notes: not traditional but gives a pleasant herby note

steps.text: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Use about 1 tsp salt per litre — the water should taste mildly of the sea.
Warm the olive oil in a wide, heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the garlic slices and cook gently for 1–2 minutes until they are pale golden and fragrant — do not let them brown.

Add all the tomatoes to the pan, raise the heat to medium-high and season with salt. Crush any large pieces with the back of a spoon. Cook uncovered, stirring occasionally, for 12–15 minutes until the sauce is thick and the oil floats on the surface.

Meanwhile, cook the pasta in the boiling water according to packet time but pull it out 1 minute early — it should be firm at the centre. Reserve a full mug (200 ml) of starchy pasta water before draining.

Add the drained pasta directly into the tomato pan. Toss briskly over medium heat, splashing in pasta water a little at a time until the sauce clings to each strand and the bottom of the pan is just moistened, about 2 minutes.
Remove from heat, tear in the basil leaves and crack over black pepper. Serve immediately — pomodoro waits for no one.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: tomato → 400 g canned crushed tomatoes (use San Marzano style if available; adjust salt accordingly); basil → fresh coriander leaves or dried oregano (not traditional but gives a pleasant herby note); olive-oil → any neutral cooking oil (olive oil adds authentic flavour but refined sunflower oil works); pasta → rice vermicelli or thin wheat noodles (boil for 3–4 minutes less; sauce quantity stays the same)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook the sauce in a kadai on medium flame — it works perfectly. If spaghetti is unavailable, use thin wheat noodles or seviyan; cook them separately and drain before adding to the sauce. Fresh basil is available in metro stores; coriander is a passable stand-in. Olive oil (extra-light variety) is sold at most large supermarkets and online grocery apps at moderate price.
